398.lessonContent: Real Time Architecture: Connection Plane vs Data Plane Separation ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

399.lessonTitle: Delivery Semantics And Resumability Sequence Numbers Acks And Replay
399.lessonContent: Delivery Semantics and Resumability: Sequence Numbers, Acks, and Replay ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

400.lessonTitle: Failure Modes Reconnect Storms Slow Consumers And TCP Head Of Line Blocking
400.lessonContent: Failure Modes: Reconnect Storms, Slow Consumers, and TCP Head of Line Blocking ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

401.lessonTitle: Protocol Alternatives And Trade Offs SSE Long Polling Http2 And Push Notifications
401.lessonContent: Protocol Alternatives and Trade Offs: SSE, Long Polling, HTTP/2, and Push Notifications ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

402.lessonTitle: Production Implementation Patterns Sharding Backpressure And Observability
402.lessonContent: Production Implementation Patterns: Sharding, Backpressure, and Observability ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

403.lessonTitle: What Is CPU Scheduling And Context Switching
403.lessonContent: What is CPU Scheduling and Context Switching? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition 💡 Key Insight: Context switch overhead is 1 to 10 microseconds directly, but cache warming after switch can cost hundreds of microseconds for memory intensive workloads. Minimizing switches is more important than making individual switches faster.

404.lessonTitle: Linux Cfs Scheduler And Production Impact
404.lessonContent: Linux CFS Scheduler and Production Impact ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: CFS prioritizes fairness over latency. A burst of CPU intensive threads increases context switch rate for all threads. For latency sensitive workloads, consider CPU isolation or real time scheduling classes.

405.lessonTitle: Kubernetes CPU Throttling And Cfs Bandwidth Control
405.lessonContent: Kubernetes CPU Throttling and CFS Bandwidth Control ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: Set CPU requests to actual steady state usage. Set limits 20 to 50 percent higher than requests to allow burst. Monitor throttling metrics. If throttling correlates with latency spikes, increase limits or consider removing them for critical services.

406.lessonTitle: 11 Threading VS Mn User Space Scheduling
406.lessonContent: 1:1 Threading vs M:N User Space Scheduling ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: M:N scheduling excels at high concurrency with many lightweight tasks. 1:1 threading works well for CPU bound workloads where kernel scheduling and preemption are needed. Choose based on your workload pattern.

407.lessonTitle: CPU Affinity Core Pinning And Numa Awareness
407.lessonContent: CPU Affinity, Core Pinning, and NUMA Awareness ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: For latency sensitive services, pin application threads to dedicated cores. Use isolcpus to prevent kernel interference. Align pinning with NUMA topology. Leave some cores for OS tasks and interrupts.

408.lessonTitle: Common Context Switching Failure Modes At Scale
408.lessonContent: Common Context Switching Failure Modes at Scale ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Size thread pools to core count for CPU bound work. Use async I/O and event loops for I/O bound work with high connection counts. Monitor context switch rate: if exceeding 10K per second per core, investigate thread count and locking.

409.lessonContent: Concurrency vs Parallelism: Core Distinction ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Core Distinction 💡 Key Insight: Concurrency is about program structure that allows interleaving. Parallelism is about hardware execution that allows simultaneity. You can have concurrency without parallelism, parallelism without concurrency, both, or neither.

410.lessonTitle: Tail Latency Amplification In Parallel Fan Out
410.lessonContent: Tail Latency Amplification in Parallel Fan Out ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Hedged requests reduce tail latency but increase backend load by 2x or more. Only use for critical paths where latency matters more than efficiency. Monitor backend capacity before enabling hedging at scale.

411.lessonContent: Concurrency Control and Bulkhead Isolation ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: Size bulkheads based on acceptable degradation. If checkout needs 100 threads and recommendations needs 50, allocate pools of 120 and 60. The 20% buffer handles bursts. Monitor pool queue depths as early warning of saturation.

412.lessonTitle: Parallelism Limits Amdahls Law And Coordination Costs
412.lessonContent: Parallelism Limits: Amdahl's Law and Coordination Costs ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: More parallelism requires more coordination. At some point, coordination cost exceeds benefit. The goal is not maximum parallelism but optimal parallelism where throughput peaks.

413.lessonTitle: Combined Strategies Production Fan Out Patterns
413.lessonContent: Combined Strategies: Production Fan Out Patterns ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: Production fan out combines multiple patterns. Scatter gather distributes work, timeouts handle stragglers, partial results ensure responsiveness, and work stealing balances load. No single pattern solves everything.

414.lessonTitle: Resource Budgeting And Failure Modes At Scale
414.lessonContent: Resource Budgeting and Failure Modes at Scale ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Implement resource budgeting early in system design. Retrofitting limits into an overloaded system is painful. Start with conservative limits and tune based on production data.

415.lessonTitle: What Is Garbage Collection And How Does It Work
415.lessonContent: What is Garbage Collection and How Does It Work? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition 💡 Key Insight: GC trades CPU overhead and pause times for memory safety. Modern GCs achieve sub-millisecond pauses for most applications. The key design dimensions are throughput, latency, and memory overhead.

416.lessonTitle: Generational And Region Based Garbage Collection
416.lessonContent: Generational and Region Based Garbage Collection ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Generational GC assumes most objects die young. Workloads that create many long lived objects (large caches, connection pools) defeat this assumption. Objects promote quickly, filling old generation and triggering expensive major GCs.

417.lessonTitle: Gc Throughput VS Latency Trade Offs And Memory Overhead
417.lessonContent: GC Throughput vs Latency Trade offs and Memory Overhead ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: Pick your priority: throughput (parallel GC), latency (concurrent GC), or ultra-low pause (ZGC or Shenandoah). No collector optimizes all dimensions. Understand your workload requirements before choosing.

418.lessonTitle: Production Gc Implementations Jvm Go Dotnet
418.lessonContent: Production GC Implementations: JVM, Go, and .NET ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: For JVM latency critical services, evaluate ZGC or Shenandoah. For Go services, tune GOGC to balance memory and CPU. For .NET, use server mode and monitor LOH size to catch fragmentation early.

419.lessonTitle: Common Gc Failure Modes And How To Prevent Them
419.lessonContent: Common GC Failure Modes and How to Prevent Them ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Object pooling reduces allocation pressure but complicates code and risks pool exhaustion or leaks. Pool only for proven hot spots with measurable improvement. Premature pooling adds complexity without benefit.

420.lessonTitle: Gc Tuning Strategy Metrics Sizing And Architectural Patterns
420.lessonContent: GC Tuning Strategy: Metrics, Sizing, and Architectural Patterns ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Start with defaults. Monitor GC metrics in production. Tune only when metrics show problems. Most applications perform well with default settings and appropriate heap sizing.

421.lessonTitle: Blocking VS Non Blocking IO Memory And Threading Trade Offs
421.lessonContent: Blocking vs Non-Blocking I/O: Memory and Threading Trade-offs ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Core Distinction 💡 Key Insight: Blocking I/O trades memory (threads) for simplicity. Non-blocking I/O trades complexity for scalability. For high connection counts, non-blocking with event loops is the only viable option.

422.lessonTitle: Synchronous VS Asynchronous Control Flow And Notification Models
422.lessonContent: Synchronous vs Asynchronous: Control Flow and Notification Models ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Async improves throughput but complicates code. Debugging async code is harder: stack traces do not show causation. Error handling must propagate through callbacks or promise chains. Use async when concurrency demands justify the complexity.

423.lessonTitle: Reactor VS Proactor Readiness VS Completion Driven IO
423.lessonContent: Reactor vs Proactor: Readiness vs Completion Driven I/O ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: Reactor says "socket is ready, do the I/O now." Proactor says "I/O is done, here is the data." Reactor is more common on Linux. Proactor (via io_uring or IOCP) reduces syscalls and improves throughput for high volume workloads.

424.lessonTitle: Event Loop Failure Modes Stalls Backpressure And Thundering Herds
424.lessonContent: Event Loop Failure Modes: Stalls, Backpressure, and Thundering Herds ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Monitor event loop lag continuously. Implement backpressure before memory exhaustion. Use EPOLLEXCLUSIVE or SO_REUSEPORT for multi-threaded accept. Test under load to find these issues before production.

425.lessonTitle: Virtual Memory Fundamentals Address Translation Process Isolation
425.lessonContent: Virtual Memory Fundamentals: Address Translation Process Isolation ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition 💡 Key Insight: Virtual memory abstracts physical memory like files abstract disk blocks. Programs use logical addresses; the OS and hardware map them to physical resources. This enables isolation, overcommit, and memory mapped files.

426.lessonTitle: Page Size Trade Offs 4 Kb VS 2 Mb VS 1 Gb
426.lessonContent: Page Size Trade-offs: 4 KB vs 2 MB vs 1 GB ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Huge pages trade memory efficiency for TLB efficiency. 2 MB pages can waste up to 2 MB per allocation but reduce TLB misses by 500x. Best for large, long lived allocations in memory bound workloads.

427.lessonTitle: Demand Paging And Page Fault Latency Impacts
427.lessonContent: Demand Paging and Page Fault Latency Impacts ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: For latency critical paths, measure page fault rates. If major faults occur on hot paths, prefault the working set at startup. Use mlock to prevent the kernel from swapping out critical pages under memory pressure.

428.lessonTitle: Memory Overcommit And Copy On Write Cow Trade Offs
428.lessonContent: Memory Overcommit and Copy-On-Write (COW) Trade-offs ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: COW makes fork fast but spreads the copy cost across subsequent writes. For write heavy workloads after fork, the cumulative fault handling can exceed what a full copy would have cost upfront.

429.lessonTitle: Numa Memory Locality And Cross Socket Access Costs
429.lessonContent: NUMA Memory Locality and Cross-Socket Access Costs ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: Memory bound applications on multi socket servers must be NUMA aware. A 2x latency penalty on 50% of accesses reduces throughput by 25% or more. Use numactl to pin workloads and numastat to monitor locality.

430.lessonTitle: Memory Failure Modes Thrashing Thp Stalls And Oom
430.lessonContent: Memory Failure Modes: Thrashing, THP Stalls, and OOM ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Disable THP for latency sensitive workloads. Set oom_score_adj to protect critical processes. Monitor page fault rates and swap usage as early warning of memory pressure. Prefer failing fast on allocation over succeeding and dying later.

431.lessonTitle: Process VS Thread Core Architecture And Isolation
431.lessonContent: Process vs Thread: Core Architecture and Isolation ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition 💡 Key Insight: The choice between processes and threads is fundamentally about trading isolation for performance. Processes give you crash containment and security boundaries. Threads give you shared memory and faster communication.

432.lessonTitle: Communication Costs Shared Memory VS Ipc
432.lessonContent: Communication Costs: Shared Memory vs IPC ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Shared memory gives nanosecond latency but requires careful synchronization. IPC gives isolation but costs microseconds per operation.

433.lessonTitle: Scaling Models Thread Pools VS Event Driven VS Process Pools
433.lessonContent: Scaling Models: Thread Pools vs Event Driven vs Process Pools ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 🎯 When To Use: Thread pools for CPU bound work with stable code. Event driven for I/O bound work with many connections. Process pools when crash isolation matters more than raw performance.

434.lessonTitle: Trade Offs Isolation VS Performance At Scale
434.lessonContent: Trade-offs: Isolation vs Performance at Scale ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ⚠️ Key Trade-off: Below 10,000 msg/s, prefer processes for isolation. Above 100,000 msg/s, threads become necessary. In between, decide based on crash frequency and security needs.

435.lessonTitle: Failure Modes Thread Explosion And Ipc Backpressure
435.lessonContent: Failure Modes: Thread Explosion and IPC Backpressure ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ 💡 Key Insight: Thread explosion and IPC backpressure both stem from unbounded resources. The fix: set limits, queue excess work, apply backpressure upstream.

436.lessonTitle: Implementation Patterns Per Core Sharding And Thread Pool Sizing
436.lessonContent: Implementation Patterns: Per Core Sharding and Thread Pool Sizing ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✅ Best Practice: Start with simple thread pools sized to core count. Measure actual wait time and service time ratios before tuning. Add per core sharding only when lock contention shows up in profiling.

437.lessonTitle: What Is Consistent Hashing And Why Does It Matter
437.lessonContent: What is Consistent Hashing and Why Does It Matter? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ✓ In Practice: Without consistent hashing, every scaling operation becomes a risky, manually coordinated migration event. With it, scaling is a routine automated action that completes in minutes, not days.

443.lessonTitle: What Is Hash Based Partitioning And Why Use It
443.lessonContent: What is Hash Based Partitioning and Why Use It? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Simple modulo hashing (key % N) causes massive data movement when N changes. Adding one node to 100 nodes remaps nearly all keys. Use consistent hashing instead.

449.lessonTitle: What Are Hotspots And Why Do They Matter
449.lessonContent: What Are Hotspots and Why Do They Matter? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ✓ In Practice: Track per-partition metrics (CPU, IOPS, queue depth), not just aggregates. A system with 10% average utilization can still have hotspots if one partition is at 100%.

455.lessonTitle: What Is Range Based Partitioning And How Does It Work
455.lessonContent: What is Range Based Partitioning and How Does It Work? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Monotonic keys like timestamps or auto-increment IDs concentrate recent writes in the highest range, creating hotspots. Time-series workloads often prefix keys with hash buckets or reverse timestamps to spread load.

461.lessonTitle: What Is Rebalancing In Distributed Systems
461.lessonContent: What is Rebalancing in Distributed Systems? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ✓ In Practice: Good rebalancing moves only ~1/N of data when adding one node to N nodes. With consistent hashing, adding one node to a 100-node cluster moves only ~1% of data, not all of it.

467.lessonTitle: What Are Document Partitioned Local Secondary Indexes
467.lessonContent: What Are Document Partitioned (Local) Secondary Indexes? ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Definition ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Probability of hitting at least one slow shard is 1 - 0.99^100 = 63%. Local secondary index queries can see p99 latencies of 50-200ms when fan-out reaches dozens of partitions, compared to single-digit milliseconds for primary key lookups.

Defense Strategy: Rotate model architectures and features periodically. Adversaries who learn current model boundaries face new detection surfaces after rotation.
